source.title: SDK: add batchingTier option (fast/standard/cost_efficient) + generated types
source.author: Jordan Lee
source.release_notes: TypeScript SDK now supports selecting the workload-aware batching tier via `batchingTier` on generation/chat requests. Supported values: `fast`, `standard`, `cost_efficient`. If omitted, server-side defaults apply.
commits: sdk(types): add BatchingTier union + plumb through request options
sdk(client): send x-redwood-batching-tier header when provided
sdk(openapi): regenerate types + update docs snippets
test: add request serialization coverage for batchingTier
source.body:
## Summary
Adds an explicit `batchingTier` option to the TS SDK so customers can opt into the new workload-aware batching SLO tiers (fast / standard / cost_efficient) on a per-request basis. This matches the python SDK addition and the server-side rollout plan (initially opt-in via header).

## API surface
- New optional request option: `batchingTier?: BatchingTier`
- `BatchingTier` is a string union: `"fast" | "standard" | "cost_efficient"`
- When set, the SDK sends `x-redwood-batching-tier: <tier>`.
- If omitted, the SDK does not send the header and server-side defaults apply.

Example:
```ts
import { Redwood } from "@redwood-inference/sdk";

const client = new Redwood({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY! });

const res = await client.chat.completions.create({
  model: "redwood/llama-3.1-70b-instruct",
  messages: [{ role: "user", content: "Summarize this…" }],
  batchingTier: "standard",
});
```

## Notes / behavior
- This is intentionally request-scoped only (no global default yet) to avoid surprising customers during rollout.
- Header name aligns with control-plane policy precedence work (customer override > route policy > tier default).
- Validation is performed client-side (compile-time via union type + runtime guard for unknown strings when using JS).

## Implementation details
- Plumbed the option through `chat.completions.create` and `generate.create` request paths.
- Added a small serializer helper so we do not emit the header when undefined.
- Regenerated `src/types/generated.ts` from the OpenAPI snapshot.

## Tests
- Added request serialization test to assert header presence/absence and exact spelling.

## Rollout
- Safe to ship independent of server runtime; server will ignore the header until feature flag is enabled.

---
### Review conversation (highlights)
**Henry Cho:** Can we avoid introducing a breaking rename (e.g., `cost-efficient` vs `cost_efficient`) and keep it consistent with backend enum? Also please add a doc snippet.

**Jordan Lee (author):** Backend enum uses `cost_efficient` (snake) for config compatibility; updated README and examples to use the same. Added a short section explaining values.

**Ethan Park:** Please confirm the header casing/format matches gateway normalization. We’ve had issues with mixed casing.

**Jordan Lee (author):** Confirmed with gateway: we normalize to lowercase on the wire; using `x-redwood-batching-tier` consistently. Added a unit test to lock it.

**Benji Okafor:** Can we ensure we don’t accidentally send an empty header when users pass `""`?

**Jordan Lee (author):** Added runtime guard: empty string is rejected with a clear error message. JS users will see `Invalid batchingTier`.

**Nikhil Sharma:** This is good. Please link to the public docs PR once it lands.

**Jordan Lee (author):** Linked in README note as TODO; will update in follow-up after docs merge.

Approvals: Henry Cho ✅, Ethan Park ✅, Benji Okafor ✅, Nikhil Sharma ✅

## Checklist
- [x] Types generated and checked in
- [x] README + example updated
- [x] Unit test added
- [x] Changeset added

Related:
- Python SDK: redwood-sdk-python#1190
- Runtime tiered defaults rollout: ENG-18422 / PM-612

source.title: Introduce delegated token channel and unified refresh for pull/push streams (ergonomic parity)
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: Motivation: There has been persistent cross-SDK divergence in how streaming consumers handle credential refresh and retries, resulting in token rotation races, duplicated refresh attempts, and awkward ergonomics when migrating examples between TypeScript, Go, and Python. This PR introduces a delegated token channel abstraction in the TypeScript SDK that matches the semantics we iterated on in Go/Python: single-flight refresh, lease-window proactive refresh, and explicit channel handoff for pull and push style streams. Summary of changes: 1) src/auth/delegated_channel.ts: new DelegatedTokenChannel class that accepts a user-provided async refresh handler and exposes a lightweight getToken()/invalidate() API optimized for streaming hot-paths. 2) src/stream/pullstream_adapter.ts and src/stream/pushstream_adapter.ts: adapters updated to consume DelegatedTokenChannel; removed inline refresh logic and consolidated retry logic into a shared stream/retry shim. 3) src/client/stream_client.ts: ergonomic convenience helpers StreamClient.openPullStreamWithDelegatedRefresh() and openPushStreamWithDelegatedRefresh() to reduce boilerplate for common patterns. 4) docs/auth/stream-credential-flow.md: documented the new pattern and migration notes. 5) tests/integration/stream_token_rotation.test.ts: deterministic test harness that simulates short-lived tokens and asserts single-flight refresh under concurrent consumers. 6) package.json bumped to 0.8.0 to reflect API additions. Compatibility and migration: The DelegatedTokenChannel is fully backward compatible with existing tokenProvider callback patterns but is recommended for streaming workloads. We maintained the legacy token callback wrapper (deprecated) and added deprecation warnings for one release cycle. Performance: stream hot-paths were benchmarked; added microbench in perf/bench_stream_token_hotpath.ts showing ~8% lower allocs and ~6% lower p95 latency when using delegated channel due to removed promise duplication. Checklist: - [x] Design doc reviewed with SDK owners (ENG-9345) - [x] Type-safe APIs with TS declarations and JSDoc - [x] Integration tests covering race conditions - [x] Docs and quickstart updated - [x] Version bump and changelog entry - [x] CI green after two reruns Test notes: initial CI run showed a flaky integration test that reproduced locally when environment timeouts were aggressive; adjusted harness timing and re-ran full suite. Observability: added telemetry events auth.refresh.requested/auth.refresh.completed for monitoring refresh count per-client to make regressions visible in Console. Rollout plan: merged to main behind feature-flag default-on for SDK 0.8.x; recommend library consumers to adopt DelegatedTokenChannel for streaming jobs and migrate examples. Related: aligns TypeScript behavior with redwood-sdk-go@v1.5.3 and redwood-sdk-python@0.12.4 token lease semantics.
commits: feat(auth): add DelegatedTokenChannel abstraction and tests
refactor(stream): adapt pull/push adapters to delegated token channel
feat(client): add ergonomic stream open helpers with delegated refresh
docs: add stream credential flow doc and migration notes
perf: add microbench for stream hot-path
test: integration harness for token rotation races
chore: bump package.json to 0.8.0
fix(ci): stabilize integration harness timings
chore: add telemetry for auth refresh events
review_comments: Lars: Nice abstraction. A few nits: can we collapse internal single-flight into src/utils/single_flight.ts and add a small benchmark?
Maya: Moved single-flight into src/utils and added microbench; clarified JSDoc on edge cases where invalidate() should be used.
Priya: Integration test looks solid. Please add one assertion verifying no more than one refresh call for 30 concurrent consumers.
Maya: Added assertion and tightened timeouts; integration test now reproduces deterministically in CI harness.
Ethan: Docs are helpful. Consider adding a short migration snippet for existing tokenProvider users.
Maya: Added migration snippet and a deprecation note for the older token callback wrapper.
CI bot: Integration test failed on first run due to flaky timer; rerun triggered and passed.
ci_checks: integration tests: failed then pass on rerun
source.merge_outcome: merged via squash on main (squash-merge). Changelog entry added to HISTORY.md and release notes for 0.8.0.
source.release_notes: TypeScript SDK 0.8.0: Introduces DelegatedTokenChannel for streaming credential refresh, ergonomic StreamClient helpers for pull/push streams, improved single-flight token refresh semantics to avoid duplicate refreshes under concurrency, performance improvements on stream hot-path, and telemetry for auth refresh events. Backward compatible; legacy token callback wrapper deprecated for one release.

source.title: feat(usage): add billed token + experiment cohort fields to Usage API client
source.author: Monica Patel
release_notes: Usage API: surface billed token fields (billedPromptTokens, billedCompletionTokens, billedTokensTotal) alongside raw token fields.
Usage API: surface experiment attribution fields (experimentKey, experimentVariant, experimentCohort) when present in the API response.
No breaking changes: new fields are optional and default to undefined.
source.body: Context: Finance/RevOps + Product are analyzing Q1 pricing/packaging experiments and hardening metering. The backend Usage API now returns additional dimensions needed for (a) fair customer explanations (billed vs raw tokens, cache attribution) and (b) experiment analysis (cohort/variant attribution). This PR updates the TypeScript SDK to expose those fields in a typed, backwards-compatible way. Changes: (1) Update Usage API response types to include raw token fields and billed token fields (rawPromptTokens/rawCompletionTokens/rawTokensTotal + billedPromptTokens/billedCompletionTokens/billedTokensTotal) and cache attribution (cacheHitTokens when returned by API). (2) Add experiment attribution fields to usage line items: experimentKey, experimentVariant, experimentCohort. These map 1:1 to the API payload when present; when the account/day wasn’t part of an experiment or the server doesn’t emit these dimensions, they remain undefined. (3) Update the usage client deserialization so snake_case API fields map to camelCase SDK fields (e.g., billed_prompt_tokens -> billedPromptTokens). (4) Update docs/snippets in the README for usage exports to call out billed vs raw tokens and the experiment fields as optional dimensions. Example usage: const rows = await client.usage.list({ start: '2026-02-01', end: '2026-02-02', groupBy: ['day','model','region'] }); for (const r of rows.data) { console.log(r.billedTokensTotal, r.rawTokensTotal, r.experimentVariant); } Backwards compatibility: additive only; existing consumers compiling against older fields continue to work. The runtime behavior is unchanged; this is purely surfacing additional fields returned by the API. Testing: added unit tests around usage response parsing (covers missing fields, partial presence, and numeric coercion), and ran the examples against staging with a known experiment account. Notes for rollout: publish as a minor version bump after the server-side Usage API field rollout is at 100% (server is already gated). Links: aligns with the ongoing metering hardening + experiment analysis work (PM-1848) and addresses customer confusion seen in SUP-28451 (invoice vs usage export mismatch) by making billed token fields first-class in the SDK.
commits: feat(usage): add billed token fields to UsageLineItem
feat(usage): add experiment attribution fields to UsageLineItem
fix(usage): map snake_case API fields to camelCase consistently
test(usage): parsing coverage for optional billed/experiment fields
docs: update usage export examples with billed vs raw tokens
review_conversation: Nadia Rahman: Can we be explicit in naming that these are billed tokens vs raw tokens? Also confirm whether billedTokensTotal includes cache-hit tokens or excludes them (depending on pricing rules).

Monica Patel (author): Updated field docs to say billed* comes from the server’s billing meter output; added note that cacheHitTokens is a separate optional field and that billedTokensTotal is authoritative for invoicing. The SDK doesn’t recompute totals.
Logan Wright: Please keep the mapping layer centralized; avoid duplicating snake_case conversion in multiple resources.
Monica Patel (author): Moved mapping to serializers.ts and added a small table-driven test so we don’t regress when new fields are added.
Chris Osei: For experiment analysis, we need cohort/variant fields exposed even if groupBy doesn’t include them (they’ll just be repeated). Does the SDK drop unknown fields today?

Monica Patel (author): SDK already preserves known fields only; added explicit typed fields for experimentKey/Variant/Cohort so they’re accessible regardless of groupBy. Unknown fields are still ignored.
Leila Farouk: Add a test case for ‘older server’ payload with none of these fields to ensure no runtime exceptions.
Monica Patel (author): Added test ‘parses legacy payload’ covering absent fields and ensuring values are undefined.
Nadia Rahman: Approved after doc clarification + serializer refactor.
Logan Wright: Approved.
Chris Osei: Approved.
source.merge_outcome: Merged to main via squash; version bump queued for next SDK release train after backend field rollout completes.

source.title: Add request ID capture + logging helper for faster Support escalations
source.author: Benji Okafor
source.release_notes:
- New helper to capture Redwood request IDs from responses/errors
- Optional request ID logging hook for easier Support handoff
- Error objects now preserve `requestId` when available (non-breaking)
source.body:
## Summary
Support escalations frequently stall because customers don’t include request IDs, or because the SDK drops the `x-request-id` header on error paths. This PR adds a small, opt-in helper that (a) captures Redwood request IDs from responses and error responses, (b) exposes them on thrown SDK errors, and (c) provides a safe logging hook so customers can copy/paste request IDs into Support tickets.

This is intentionally not enabled by default to avoid surprising logging behavior in customer apps.

## Motivation
- Support intake + escalation playbook now standardizes on collecting request IDs early (see linked SUP tickets).
- Streaming disconnects/timeouts are hard to reproduce without request IDs.
- Customers often ask “where do I find the request ID?” and we want a canonical answer in the TS SDK.

## What changed
- Added `requestId` extraction utilities (from headers) and an opt-in hook `onRequestId`.
- Updated SDK error shaping so thrown errors preserve a `requestId` field when present.
- Updated docs and examples to show how to log/capture request IDs without logging full payloads.

### Public API
- `getRequestIdFromHeaders(headers: Headers | Record<string, string | string[] | undefined>): string | undefined`
- `createRequestIdLogger({ onRequestId })` -> returns a small helper to attach to SDK request pipeline

No behavior changes unless consumers opt in by passing `onRequestId`.

## Implementation notes
- We normalize header lookups for Node (undici / fetch) and browser environments.
- We parse request ID from:
  - `x-request-id` (canonical)
  - `x-redwood-request-id` (legacy alias; kept for compatibility)
- Errors thrown by the SDK now include:
  - `requestId?: string`
  - `status?: number`

source.title: Retry-After parsing (delta-seconds + HTTP-date) + jittered exponential backoff
source.author: Dana Lo
source.description: Context: As part of the cross-language SDK parity initiative (retry/timeout standard), TS currently only parses Retry-After when it is a delta-seconds value. The gateway and some upstreams also emit Retry-After as an HTTP-date (e.g. "Wed, 21 Oct 2015 07:28:00 GMT"), which we should respect to avoid retrying too aggressively during throttling. In addition, our retry middleware used a deterministic exponential backoff sequence, which can create synchronized retries (thundering herd) across many clients. This PR adds: (1) Retry-After parsing for both delta-seconds and HTTP-date formats, (2) jittered exponential backoff consistent with the parity standard, and (3) tests/fixtures to lock behavior. Behavior details: Retry-After parsing: If header is delta-seconds => wait = clamp(deltaSeconds*1000, 0..maxRetryAfterMs). If header is HTTP-date => wait = clamp(dateMs - nowMs, 0..maxRetryAfterMs). If parsing fails or results in NaN/negative => treat as absent and fall back to exponential backoff. We only honor Retry-After on status codes where we retry (429, 500/502/503/504), with primary focus on 429. Backoff + jitter: Introduce a jittered exponential backoff helper (baseDelayMs=250, multiplier=2, maxDelayMs=10_000) using full jitter: delay = randomInt(0..cap) where cap = min(maxDelayMs, baseDelayMs * 2^(attempt-1)). This matches the parity guidance and reduces retry synchronization under load. Backwards compatibility: No API surface changes; retry defaults remain the same maxAttempts. Only changes are (a) respecting additional valid Retry-After values and (b) adding jitter to the sleep, which may slightly change exact retry timing but improves reliability at scale. Testing: Adds unit tests for Retry-After parsing (seconds, HTTP-date, invalid, negative/0, far-future clamp) and for backoff jitter bounds. Also adds an integration-style test against the retry middleware with a simulated 429 + Retry-After HTTP-date to validate we wait at least the computed duration (with deterministic clock + stubbed RNG). Notes: This does not change streaming retry behavior (still conservative) and does not add any telemetry fields; those are tracked separately under ENG-5129.
commits: refactor(retry): extract retry delay calculation into helper
feat(retry): parse Retry-After as delta-seconds or HTTP-date (RFC-compliant-ish)
feat(retry): add full-jitter exponential backoff with cap
test(retry): add unit tests for retry-after parsing and jitter bounds
test(retry): add middleware test for 429 + Retry-After HTTP-date
changed_files: src/retry/backoff.ts: implement full-jitter exponential backoff helper
src/middleware/retry.ts: use parsed Retry-After when present; otherwise jittered backoff
src/core/httpClient.ts: thread clock/now provider for tests (default Date.now)
test/retry/backoff.test.ts: jitter bounds and cap behavior
docs/CHANGELOG.md: note Retry-After date + jitter change
package.json: bump dev dep for deterministic RNG in tests
conversation: Allison Grant (review): Can we confirm we handle both Retry-After formats (delta-seconds + HTTP-date) and clamp to a reasonable maximum? We should avoid sleeping for hours if the server sends a bad date.
Dana Lo (author): Yep. maxRetryAfterMs default is 60_000 (same as other SDKs). For HTTP-date we compute date-now and clamp to [0, maxRetryAfterMs]. Invalid parses fall back to normal backoff.
Ibrahim Khan (review): Please ensure negative/zero values don’t end up as a large unsigned delay somewhere. Also, can you avoid depending on Date parsing quirks across runtimes?

Dana Lo (author): Added explicit checks for NaN and <0. For HTTP-date we use Date.parse which is what node/fetch environments support; if parse fails we treat as absent. Also added a test covering invalid strings and negative computed delays.
Sean Gallagher (review): +1 on jitter. Which jitter algorithm did we settle on in the standard? Full jitter vs equal jitter vs decorrelated?

Dana Lo (author): Implemented full jitter (random 0..cap). That’s what we’ve been recommending in the parity standard to reduce synchronization. Added tests to ensure bounds and cap behavior are correct.
Allison Grant (review): Requesting change: honor Retry-After only on retryable codes; don’t accidentally sleep on 400s if someone toggles retryable status codes in the future.
Dana Lo (author): Updated middleware to only consult Retry-After after classification says retryable. Added a regression test that a 400 with Retry-After does not wait/retry.
Ibrahim Khan (review): Looks good. One minor: the integration test stubs Math.random globally; can we scope it to the helper?
Dana Lo (author): Adjusted helper to accept an optional rng() in test-only path and removed the global stub. CI now green.
Ibrahim Khan (review): Approved.
Allison Grant (review): Approved.
Sean Gallagher (review): Approved.

source.title: Unify retry middleware and error shapes (request_id + retryable) with SDK standard
source.author: Dana Lo
source.release_notes: TypeScript SDK: retry behavior now follows the SDK standard (no retries on 4xx other than 408/409/429). Errors now consistently include request_id and a retryable flag to support debugging and parity with Python/Go.
source.description:
Context
We’re in the middle of the SDK parity initiative and TS currently differs from Python/Go in two customer-visible ways:
1) Retry classification: TS retry middleware has been overly permissive (observed retries on some terminal 4xx), which contributed to SUP-29438 (duplicate billing concern).
2) Error surfaces: request_id is inconsistently propagated (INT-1182), and error types differ between streaming/non-streaming paths.

This PR refactors the retry middleware and error construction so TS matches the standard taxonomy and aligns retryable status codes. Retry-After parsing/backoff tuning will be handled in follow-up PRs (see PR-1307).

What changed
- Introduced a single error normalization layer used by both streaming + non-streaming requests:
  - All thrown errors extend RedwoodError and include:
    - requestId (from x-redwood-request-id/x-request-id header, with body fallback)
    - statusCode (when available)
    - code (gateway error code when present)
    - retryable (computed)
- Refactored retry middleware to depend on the normalized error shape instead of ad-hoc checks.
- Updated retryable classification to match the SDK retry/timeout standard:
  - Retryable: 408, 409, 429, 500–599 (excluding 501 by default; see note below)
  - Non-retryable: other 4xx (400/401/403/404/etc)
  - Network/transport errors are retryable (DNS, connection reset, TLS handshake, fetch abort not initiated by user)
- Request ID propagation:
  - Successful responses: the Response wrapper exposes requestId.
  - Error responses: requestId is extracted from headers first, then JSON body fields (request_id/requestId) if present.
- Observability hooks:
  - Retry loop now increments a local attempt counter and attaches it to the error as metadata when we eventually give up.
  - Ensures we do NOT include the Authorization header or API key in any error message (Security review guidance).

Notes / decisions
- 501 handling: The standard doc calls out 5xx generally as retryable; SRE feedback was to treat 501 as non-retryable by default (usually indicates unsupported feature/method rather than transient). This PR implements that as a conservative default; we can revisit once gateway error codes are fully consistent.
- Abort semantics: When the caller’s AbortSignal triggers, we now classify it as non-retryable and preserve the original AbortError name/message.

Testing
- Added unit tests for shouldRetry(): verifies status code matrix and network error classification.
- Added tests ensuring request_id is extracted from:
  - response headers (preferred)
  - JSON error body (fallback)
- Updated existing integration tests to assert error.retryable and error.requestId are present.

Rollout
- This should be non-breaking for most customers: existing code catching Error will continue to work.
- Some customers may have relied on the previous (incorrect) retry-on-400 behavior; this change is intentional and documented in migration notes (tracked in ENG-5128).

Follow-ups
- Retry-After parsing + backoff jitter alignment (PR-1307)
- Timeout defaults + AbortController plumbing (PR-1302)
- Conformance suite fixtures (ENG-5127)

Screenshots/log samples
- Example error (sanitized):
  name=RedwoodAPIError statusCode=429 retryable=true requestId=req_01HR9Q6K9YQG3H2R9M4J7V0Z1A code=RATE_LIMITED
commits: refactor(errors): add RedwoodError base and normalized error builder
refactor(retry): centralize shouldRetry() and remove ad-hoc status checks
feat(observability): propagate requestId and attach retry metadata
test(retry): add matrix tests for retryable status codes and abort behavior
chore(docs): inline JSDoc updates for new error fields
source.conversation:
Ibrahim Khan (review) — 2026-01-30: “Thanks. Two asks for parity: (1) ensure request_id comes from headers first (gateway guarantees) and only fall back to body; (2) confirm we never retry 400/401/403/404. Also, can you align naming with other SDKs (requestId vs request_id)?”
Dana Lo (author) — 2026-01-30: “Ack. Updated extraction order to header-first; added tests. Keeping JS field as requestId but mapping both request_id/requestId from body; docstring calls out normalization.”

Allison Grant (review) — 2026-01-31: “On retryable codes: do we treat 409 as retryable? Standard says yes (conflicts can be transient with idempotency). Please add a note for customers and make sure retryable is surfaced on the error.”
Dana Lo — 2026-01-31: “Added 409 retryable and explicit test. error.retryable is now always defined for RedwoodError subclasses.”

Marcus Lin (security review) — 2026-02-01: “Please double-check no Authorization header contents end up in error.message or debug logs; also ensure requestId is safe to log.”
Dana Lo — 2026-02-01: “Removed inclusion of request headers from error messages. requestId is explicitly allowed (non-secret) and helps support triage.”

Ethan Park (platform) — 2026-02-02: “Gateway sometimes returns x-request-id vs x-redwood-request-id depending on path. Good that you check both. Can you also check body field request_id for OpenAI-compat?”
Dana Lo — 2026-02-02: “Yep, body fallback supports request_id/requestId. Added test for OpenAI-compat style payload.”

Ibrahim Khan (approval) — 2026-02-03: “Looks good. This closes the SUP-29438 class of retry misclassification for TS. Approved.”

- Some customers may have relied on the previous (incorrect) retry-on-400 behavior; this change is intentional and documented in migration notes (tracked in ENG-5128).

Follow-ups
- Retry-After parsing + backoff jitter alignment (PR-1307)
- Timeout defaults + AbortController plumbing (PR-1302)
- Conformance suite fixtures (ENG-5127)

Screenshots/log samples

  name=RedwoodAPIError statusCode=429 retryable=true requestId=req_01HR9Q6K9YQG3H2R9M4J7V0Z1A code=RATE_LIMITED
text:
Context
We’re in the middle of the SDK parity initiative and TS currently differs from Python/Go in two customer-visible ways:
1) Retry classification: TS retry middleware has been overly permissive (observed retries on some terminal 4xx), which contributed to SUP-29438 (duplicate billing concern).
2) Error surfaces: request_id is inconsistently propagated (INT-1182), and error types differ between streaming/non-streaming paths.

This PR refactors the retry middleware and error construction so TS matches the standard taxonomy and aligns retryable status codes. Retry-After parsing/backoff tuning will be handled in follow-up PRs (see PR-1307).

What changed
- Introduced a single error normalization layer used by both streaming + non-streaming requests:
  - All thrown errors extend RedwoodError and include:
    - requestId (from x-redwood-request-id/x-request-id header, with body fallback)
    - statusCode (when available)
    - code (gateway error code when present)
    - retryable (computed)
- Refactored retry middleware to depend on the normalized error shape instead of ad-hoc checks.
- Updated retryable classification to match the SDK retry/timeout standard:
  - Retryable: 408, 409, 429, 500–599 (excluding 501 by default; see note below)
  - Non-retryable: other 4xx (400/401/403/404/etc)
  - Network/transport errors are retryable (DNS, connection reset, TLS handshake, fetch abort not initiated by user)
- Request ID propagation:
  - Successful responses: the Response wrapper exposes requestId.
  - Error responses: requestId is extracted from headers first, then JSON body fields (request_id/requestId) if present.
- Observability hooks:
  - Retry loop now increments a local attempt counter and attaches it to the error as metadata when we eventually give up.
  - Ensures we do NOT include the Authorization header or API key in any error message (Security review guidance).

Notes / decisions
- 501 handling: The standard doc calls out 5xx generally as retryable; SRE feedback was to treat 501 as non-retryable by default (usually indicates unsupported feature/method rather than transient). This PR implements that as a conservative default; we can revisit once gateway error codes are fully consistent.
- Abort semantics: When the caller’s AbortSignal triggers, we now classify it as non-retryable and preserve the original AbortError name/message.

Testing
- Added unit tests for shouldRetry(): verifies status code matrix and network error classification.
- Added tests ensuring request_id is extracted from:
  - response headers (preferred)
  - JSON error body (fallback)
- Updated existing integration tests to assert error.retryable and error.requestId are present.

Rollout
- This should be non-breaking for most customers: existing code catching Error will continue to work.
- Some customers may have relied on the previous (incorrect) retry-on-400 behavior; this change is intentional and documented in migration notes (tracked in ENG-5128).

Follow-ups
- Retry-After parsing + backoff jitter alignment (PR-1307)
- Timeout defaults + AbortController plumbing (PR-1302)
- Conformance suite fixtures (ENG-5127)

- Example error (sanitized):

source.title: sdk(auth): align env var names + precedence with Python/Go (deprecate legacy names)
source.author: Dana Lo
source.description: Summary: Aligns TS SDK authentication environment variables and resolution precedence with the Python and Go SDKs as part of the SDK parity initiative. This change also introduces deprecation warnings for legacy env var names to reduce "works in TS but not in Python" issues and to standardize support/debugging instructions. Motivation: Support + SE feedback shows customers frequently set different env vars per language and are surprised by which value wins when both are present. We’re standardizing on a single set of env var names and a single precedence order across SDKs, with security-reviewed guidance to avoid leaking secrets in logs/errors. New behavior (matches Python/Go): Precedence order: (1) explicit client option (apiKey / bearerToken) (2) environment variables (standard names) (3) none -> throw a typed auth error. Standard env vars supported: REDWOOD_API_KEY for API key auth; REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN for bearer token auth; REDWOOD_BASE_URL remains as-is for endpoint override. Legacy env vars (still accepted, now deprecated with a warning): RW_API_KEY, REDWOOD_API_TOKEN, REDWOOD_TOKEN. If multiple env vars are set: explicit client option always wins; between env vars we prefer REDWOOD_API_KEY over deprecated key vars; bearer token is only used when api key is not present (same as other SDKs). Deprecation warnings: Warnings are emitted once per process (guarded) and do not print secret values. We include guidance text pointing to the migration notes and the new standard names. Error handling: Missing credentials now throws RedwoodAuthError with message safe for logs; error includes "source" metadata (explicit/env/none) but never includes the credential string. Tests: Adds unit coverage for precedence, deprecation warning behavior, and non-leakage. Also updates the README auth snippet to reference REDWOOD_API_KEY / REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN consistently. Notes: This PR intentionally does not change header formats beyond existing behavior (Authorization: Bearer ...). This also keeps backwards compat: legacy env vars will continue to work for at least 2 minor versions; removal will be tracked separately after customers have had time to migrate. Related: ENG-5122 (env var alignment), INT-1182 (better error metadata / support triage).
commits: sdk(auth): introduce AuthResolver and normalize credential sources
sdk(auth): support REDWOOD_API_KEY + REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN with parity precedence
sdk(auth): accept legacy env vars w/ one-time deprecation warnings
tests(auth): add coverage for precedence + warning emission + redaction
docs(readme): update auth examples to standard env vars
source.changed_files: High-level changes: packages/sdk/src/auth/resolveAuth.ts (new centralized resolver with explicit/env precedence); packages/sdk/src/client.ts (wire auth resolver into client construction and error surface); packages/sdk/src/errors.ts (add RedwoodAuthError w/ safe messaging + source metadata); packages/sdk/src/constants/env.ts (standard + legacy env var names and warning text); packages/sdk/test/auth/resolveAuth.test.ts (new tests); README.md (auth snippet + env var names). Small illustrative snippet (resolve order): `if (opts.apiKey || opts.bearerToken) return { kind: 'explicit', ... }; const apiKey = env('REDWOOD_API_KEY') ?? envLegacy('RW_API_KEY'); const bearer = env('REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN') ?? envLegacy('REDWOOD_TOKEN'); if (apiKey) return { kind: 'env', scheme: 'api_key' }; if (bearer) return { kind: 'env', scheme: 'bearer' }; throw new RedwoodAuthError('Missing Redwood credentials. Set REDWOOD_API_KEY or REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN, or pass apiKey/bearerToken explicitly.');`
source.ci_checks: CI summary (GitHub Actions): lint (pass), typecheck (pass), unit tests (pass), build (pass), bundle-size (pass). Initial run failed once due to a flaky "bundle-size" upload step; rerun succeeded with no code changes.
source.review_conversation: Ibrahim Khan (requested changes): "Can we ensure the precedence matches the draft standard exactly? Specifically explicit param > env var, and within env vars prefer standard names over legacy. Also please make the deprecation warning one-time; we don't want spam in server logs." Dana Lo (author): "Updated resolver to prefer standard vars and added a `warnOnce()` guard keyed by env var name. Added tests for both." Marcus Lin (security): "Please confirm we never log the actual secret or include it in thrown errors, even in debug mode. Also avoid printing the full env var value in deprecation warnings." Dana Lo: "Confirmed; warnings reference only the env var name. Added regression test asserting error messages do not contain any provided credential string." Sabrina James (Python/examples): "Can you align naming with Python: REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN (not ACCESS_TOKEN)?" Dana Lo: "Yes, updated to REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN and marked older TS-only aliases deprecated." Mateusz Zielinski (Go): "In Go we treat bearer token as fallback if API key is present. This matches your current approach—thanks. One nit: can we include a short note in the warning pointing to migration notes?" Dana Lo: "Added link text to docs path and kept it generic for published docs." Final approvals: Ibrahim Khan (approved), Marcus Lin (approved after security nit), Sabrina James (approved), Mateusz Zielinski (approved).
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash to main by Dana Lo. Squash commit message: "sdk(auth): align env vars + precedence; deprecate legacy names". No rollout flags; behavior is backwards compatible due to legacy env var support + warnings.
source.release_notes: TypeScript SDK: Auth env vars are now standardized with Python/Go. Use REDWOOD_API_KEY or REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN. Legacy env vars (RW_API_KEY, REDWOOD_API_TOKEN, REDWOOD_TOKEN) still work but will emit a one-time deprecation warning and may be removed in a future minor release.

source.title: Expose typed overload error classes + Retry-After handling docs
source.author: Ibrahim Khan
source.release_notes: TypeScript SDK now surfaces overload-related responses as typed errors (with parsed error code and Retry-After). Added documentation snippets for recommended client retry/backoff behavior.
source.body:
Context: As part of the Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding rollout, the API is returning stable overload semantics (HTTP 429/503 with a JSON error payload + correlation IDs + Retry-After guidance). In the TS SDK we currently throw a generic ApiError, which forces customers to string-match messages and often leads to unsafe retry loops.

This PR adds typed overload error classes and doc snippets so client code can (a) branch on stable error codes and (b) implement bounded retries with jitter that respects Retry-After.

What changed: 1) New error types in the SDK: - OverloadError (base) extends ApiError and exposes: status, code, message, correlationId, requestId, retryAfterSeconds (parsed from header), and rawPayload (best-effort). - RateLimitedError: thrown for HTTP 429 when error code indicates throttling / quota / concurrency cap. - ServiceOverloadedError: thrown for HTTP 503 when error code indicates capacity / shed / breaker open.

2) Error parsing: - Parse standard Redwood error payload fields when present: error.code, error.message, error.correlation_id, error.request_id. - Parse Retry-After header (seconds). If missing/invalid, retryAfterSeconds is undefined; the docs recommend a safe client-side backoff floor.

3) Docs + examples: - Added a short “Retry contract” section to docs with a concrete example of exponential backoff + full jitter, respecting Retry-After when provided. - Explicit guidance to avoid retry storms: cap retries, add jitter, and do not immediately retry streaming requests without backoff.

4) Tests: - Unit tests for mapping status + error.code -> typed error class. - Coverage for Retry-After parsing, including header absent and malformed cases.

Notes: - This PR is intentionally additive and does not change the default request retry behavior of the SDK (we don’t auto-retry by default). - The error code set aligns with the overload taxonomy work (see ENG-4822). We treat unknown overload-ish 429/503 as the base OverloadError to keep forward compatibility.

Customer-facing example:
try { await client.chat.completions.create(...); } catch (err) { if (err instanceof ServiceOverloadedError) { const delay = err.retryAfterSeconds ? err.retryAfterSeconds * 1000 : 250; await sleep(jitter(delay)); /* bounded retry */ } }

Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Consider adding an optional built-in retry helper in the SDK (behind a flag) once the API rollout stabilizes in more regions. - Add an “idempotency key” example for non-streaming requests once the API docs are updated.
commits: sdk/errors: add OverloadError base + subclasses for 429/503
sdk/http: map response payload + Retry-After header into error instances
docs: add overload error handling + bounded retry/backoff snippet
tests: validate error mapping + Retry-After parsing
conversation: Ethan Park (review): Can we ensure we’re not hard-coding a brittle list of codes? The gateway may add more granular overload reasons during rollout.

Ibrahim Khan (author): Yep — implemented as: if status in {429,503} and payload has error.code => map known ones to subclasses, else fall back to OverloadError with code preserved. Unknown codes remain inspectable without breaking parsing.
Nikhil Sharma (review): Please make the docs explicitly say “do not hammer retries” and to respect Retry-After. Also call out that 503 overload is not a generic outage; it’s intentional shedding.

Ibrahim Khan (author): Updated docs wording: “overload responses indicate intentional shedding; clients should back off and retry only when appropriate” + bounded retries + jitter + max attempts recommendation.
Dev Patel (review): Support would like correlationId surfaced prominently. Can we add it to toString() or error message formatting so it shows up in logs by default?

Ibrahim Khan (author): Added correlationId/requestId to OverloadError#toString() and ensured they appear in error.inspect in Node. Also added a docs sentence: “include correlation_id when contacting support.”
Wei Chen (review): Minor docs nit: make sure header is called Retry-After (case-insensitive) and clarify units are seconds.
Ibrahim Khan (author): Done — parser treats header case-insensitively via fetch Headers, assumes integer seconds per RFC; docs now explicitly state seconds.

source.title: Compact connection throttle + async iterable sugar; cross-SDK retry presets and version bump
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: TypeScript SDK 2.4.0: added compact connection throttling, ergonomic async iterable helpers, new retry presets and coordinated instructions for Python/Go sync; no breaking API changes; bump minor versions for cross-SDK parity.
source.description:
Context: Customer workloads and internal benchmarks showed bursty micro-batch patterns causing head-of-line connection contention and surprising ordering latency on long-lived streaming consumers. This PR introduces a compact connection throttle (lightweight per-host connection window + soft drop) in the TypeScript SDK, a set of ergonomic async-iterable helpers for streaming consumers, and a curated set of retry/backoff presets to align behavior across TS/Python/Go. The changes are intentionally additive and backwards-compatible for common call patterns; an opt-in config surface is provided for teams wanting deterministic throttle windows and pre-warmed connectors.

Motivation: Reduce tail latency spikes during high-concurrency streaming usage, simplify stream consumer code across codebases, and provide consistent retry semantics across SDKs so server-side rollouts and client-side fallbacks behave similarly. ENG-1987 captures the production regression we observed; ENG-2123 is the follow-up to standardize retry profiles. SUP-1245 required pragmatic changes in TS first to unblock a customer using mixed-language microservices.

What this PR does (summary):
- Adds a compact connection throttle implemented at the connection pool layer: light-weight token window per-origin, soft-drop policy when window exhausted, and optional jittered refill. Default disabled; opt-in via ClientConfig.compactConnectionThrottle.
- Adds asyncIterable.fromStream and asyncIterable.mapAsync helpers that normalize chunk framing, apply backpressure-aware reads, and expose cancelable iteration semantics. These reduce user boilerplate for streaming handlers.
- Introduces three retry presets: fast-retry, balanced, and conservative, with documented token-based default backoffs and idempotency guidance. Presets are available on ClientConfig.retryProfile.
- Adds version bump notes and a migration guide (docs/migration-2.4.0.md) with suggested changes for app teams and Python/Go sync checklist.
- Unit/integration tests for throttle behavior, iterator cancellation, and retry preset selection.

Files changed (high-level):
- src/connection/compactThrottle.ts (new)
- src/connection/pool.ts (minor refactor + opt-in hooks)
- src/stream/asyncIterable.ts (new)
- src/client/config.ts (new options)
- src/retry/presets.ts (new)
- docs/migration-2.4.0.md (new)
- test/unit/compactThrottle.test.ts (new)
- test/integration/streamCancellation.test.ts (new)
- package.json (version -> 2.4.0)
- README and changelog updates

Commits (summary):
- feat(connection): compact connection throttle implementation and integration tests
- feat(stream): asyncIterable helpers (fromStream, mapAsync, withCancel)
- feat(retry): add retry presets and docs
- feat(config): exposed ClientConfig.compactConnectionThrottle & retryProfile
- docs: migration guide and examples that show cross-SDK parity guidance
- test: end-to-end test that reproduces pre-fix tail latency pattern

Review notes and decisions:
- Throttle default: disabled. We decided to make the throttle opt-in because some customers rely on aggressive client-side concurrency; enabling by default would risk impacting throughput for those cases. If downstream service agrees, we can flip to on in a later minor.
- Jitter and refill params: conservative defaults to avoid synchronized refills; teams can tune via ClientConfig.compactConnectionThrottle.windowMs and refillJitter.
- Retry presets documented with explicit mapping guidance for Python/Go equivalents (see migration doc). The intention is that each language exposes the same three presets with comparable timings; Python/Go PR checklists are included so maintainers can port values without surprises.

Test and CI: All unit tests and integration tests in the TS repo pass locally and in CI. The PR ran the following checks: lint, build, unit, integration, e2e-stream, and cross-sdk-ci (lightweight smoke). A flaky e2e-stream test failed on first CI run (timing-related); I adjusted test timeouts and added a deterministic clock for the failing test, then retriggered CI. Final CI status: pass.

Review conversation (high level):
- Luca: asked for clearer failure semantics when throttle soft-drops chunks; requested that errors surface with a typed ThrottleError so consumers can decide whether to retry or fallback.
- Maya (author): implemented ThrottleError with code 'E_COMPACT_THROTTLE' and added a short section in the docs describing recommended handling.
- Priya: requested API ergonomics for asyncIterable.mapAsync to accept both sync and async map functions and to preserve chunk identity when map throws. Priya also wanted better cancellation signaling across underlying WS/socket backpressure.
- Maya: updated mapAsync to accept both and added withCancel wrapper to propagate cancellation to upstream socket reads.
- Ethan: validated presets and asked for an explicit callout that these presets are best-effort and servers retain final authority.

Cross-SDK coordination: This PR is the TypeScript implementation. I included a migration checklist and a JSON spec file (docs/compat/retry-presets-2.4.0.json) that describes the three presets (timeouts, multipliers, maxAttempts) to make it straightforward for Python and Go maintainers to port. I communicated this to the SDK channels and opened companion PR templates for redwood-sdk-go and redwood-sdk-python listing the files to change and tests to add. Those companion PRs are referenced in the migration doc and targeted for the 2.4.0 family.

Backward compatibility and stability: No public API surface was removed. The new helpers and throttle are additive; default behavior remains unchanged unless a team opts in to the throttle or selects a retry preset. Tests include round-trip examples that ensure existing simple usages (client.chat.generate, simple streaming consumers) behave identically.

Manual test checklist performed before merge:
- Local integration harness with 100 concurrent stream consumers verified no regressions in throughput for non-throttle config.
- Enabled compact throttle in a staging deploy and reproduced reduced tail P99 for artificial burst test.
- Verified asyncIterable cancellation closes underlying socket and frees KV caches.
- Verified packaged SDK bump in an internal demo service compiled and ran.

Merge outcome: Squash merged to main after final approvals from Luca and Priya. CI green at merge.

Follow-ups and rollout plan:
- Open follow-up to discuss default-on strategy after customer telemetry (ENG-2190).
- Coordinate Python/Go PRs to land equivalent presets and iterator helpers (owners: @go-sdk-team, @py-sdk-team).
- Add telemetry for throttle drop events to Console as a low-volume metric (SRE/observability ticket included in the migration doc).

This PR introduces a compact connection throttle (lightweight per-host connection window + soft drop) in the TypeScript SDK, a set of ergonomic async-iterable helpers for streaming consumers, and a curated set of retry/backoff presets to align behavior across TS/Python/Go. The changes are intentionally additive and backwards-compatible for common call patterns; an opt-in config surface is provided for teams wanting deterministic throttle windows and pre-warmed connectors.

Motivation: Reduce tail latency spikes during high-concurrency streaming usage, simplify stream consumer code across codebases, and provide consistent retry semantics across SDKs so server-side rollouts and client-side fallbacks behave similarly. ENG-1987 captures the production regression we observed; ENG-2123 is the follow-up to standardize retry profiles. SUP-1245 required pragmatic changes in TS first to unblock a customer using mixed-language microservices.

What this PR does (summary):
- Adds a compact connection throttle implemented at the connection pool layer: light-weight token window per-origin, soft-drop policy when window exhausted, and optional jittered refill. Default disabled; opt-in via ClientConfig.compactConnectionThrottle.
- Adds asyncIterable.fromStream and asyncIterable.mapAsync helpers that normalize chunk framing, apply backpressure-aware reads, and expose cancelable iteration semantics. These reduce user boilerplate for streaming handlers.
- Introduces three retry presets: fast-retry, balanced, and conservative, with documented token-based default backoffs and idempotency guidance. Presets are available on ClientConfig.retryProfile.
- Adds version bump notes and a migration guide (docs/migration-2.4.0.md) with suggested changes for app teams and Python/Go sync checklist.
- Unit/integration tests for throttle behavior, iterator cancellation, and retry preset selection.

Files changed (high-level):
- src/connection/compactThrottle.ts (new)
- src/connection/pool.ts (minor refactor + opt-in hooks)
- src/stream/asyncIterable.ts (new)
- src/client/config.ts (new options)
- src/retry/presets.ts (new)
- docs/migration-2.4.0.md (new)
- test/unit/compactThrottle.test.ts (new)
- test/integration/streamCancellation.test.ts (new)
- package.json (version -> 2.4.0)
- README and changelog updates

Commits (summary):
- feat(connection): compact connection throttle implementation and integration tests
- feat(stream): asyncIterable helpers (fromStream, mapAsync, withCancel)
- feat(retry): add retry presets and docs
- feat(config): exposed ClientConfig.compactConnectionThrottle & retryProfile
- docs: migration guide and examples that show cross-SDK parity guidance
- test: end-to-end test that reproduces pre-fix tail latency pattern

Review notes and decisions:
- Throttle default: disabled. We decided to make the throttle opt-in because some customers rely on aggressive client-side concurrency; enabling by default would risk impacting throughput for those cases. If downstream service agrees, we can flip to on in a later minor.
- Jitter and refill params: conservative defaults to avoid synchronized refills; teams can tune via ClientConfig.compactConnectionThrottle.windowMs and refillJitter.
- Retry presets documented with explicit mapping guidance for Python/Go equivalents (see migration doc). The intention is that each language exposes the same three presets with comparable timings; Python/Go PR checklists are included so maintainers can port values without surprises.

Test and CI: All unit tests and integration tests in the TS repo pass locally and in CI. The PR ran the following checks: lint, build, unit, integration, e2e-stream, and cross-sdk-ci (lightweight smoke). A flaky e2e-stream test failed on first CI run (timing-related); I adjusted test timeouts and added a deterministic clock for the failing test, then retriggered CI. Final CI status: pass.

Review conversation (high level):
- Luca: asked for clearer failure semantics when throttle soft-drops chunks; requested that errors surface with a typed ThrottleError so consumers can decide whether to retry or fallback.
- Maya (author): implemented ThrottleError with code 'E_COMPACT_THROTTLE' and added a short section in the docs describing recommended handling.
- Priya: requested API ergonomics for asyncIterable.mapAsync to accept both sync and async map functions and to preserve chunk identity when map throws. Priya also wanted better cancellation signaling across underlying WS/socket backpressure.
- Maya: updated mapAsync to accept both and added withCancel wrapper to propagate cancellation to upstream socket reads.
- Ethan: validated presets and asked for an explicit callout that these presets are best-effort and servers retain final authority.

Cross-SDK coordination: This PR is the TypeScript implementation. I included a migration checklist and a JSON spec file (docs/compat/retry-presets-2.4.0.json) that describes the three presets (timeouts, multipliers, maxAttempts) to make it straightforward for Python and Go maintainers to port. I communicated this to the SDK channels and opened companion PR templates for redwood-sdk-go and redwood-sdk-python listing the files to change and tests to add. Those companion PRs are referenced in the migration doc and targeted for the 2.4.0 family.

Backward compatibility and stability: No public API surface was removed. The new helpers and throttle are additive; default behavior remains unchanged unless a team opts in to the throttle or selects a retry preset. Tests include round-trip examples that ensure existing simple usages (client.chat.generate, simple streaming consumers) behave identically.

Manual test checklist performed before merge:
- Local integration harness with 100 concurrent stream consumers verified no regressions in throughput for non-throttle config.
- Enabled compact throttle in a staging deploy and reproduced reduced tail P99 for artificial burst test.
- Verified asyncIterable cancellation closes underlying socket and frees KV caches.
- Verified packaged SDK bump in an internal demo service compiled and ran.

Merge outcome: Squash merged to main after final approvals from Luca and Priya. CI green at merge.

Follow-ups and rollout plan:
- Open follow-up to discuss default-on strategy after customer telemetry (ENG-2190).
- Coordinate Python/Go PRs to land equivalent presets and iterator helpers (owners: @go-sdk-team, @py-sdk-team).
- Add telemetry for throttle drop events to Console as a low-volume metric (SRE/observability ticket included in the migration doc).

source.title: introduce-compact-auth-orchestrator-and-multi-sdk-stream-ergonomics
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.release_notes: Adds a compact auth orchestrator shared interface across TS/Py/Go SDKs; standardizes pull-stream ergonomics; bumps TS SDK to v1.8.0, Py SDK to v0.3.2 and Go SDK to v1.5.1. Backwards compatible; minor migration notes included.
source.description: Motivation: multiple SDKs had divergent credential-warmup and stream-consumer ergonomics which leaked complexity into application code. This PR introduces a compact auth orchestrator interface and runtime shim that 1) centralizes credential warmup/prefetch logic, 2) exposes a minimal async pull-stream ergonomic facade across TypeScript/ Python/ Go and 3) ships a set of opinionated retry presets tuned for low-latency streaming use-cases. The change is intended to reduce duplicate logic in application-level code and improve parity across SDKs. Summary of changes: -- Introduce compact auth orchestrator (ts): src/auth/orchestrator.ts, with a light-weight in-memory cache, lease hints, and prefetch hooks for token providers. The orchestrator exposes a small surface used by streaming connectors and session facades. -- Add pullstream ergonomic facade (ts): src/stream/pullstream.ts and public exports in lib/index.ts. This provides a simpler async iterator consumer with built-in backoff and checkpoint hints. -- Cross-SDK compatibility: added step-by-step shim examples in examples/multiproto-auth/ demonstrating TypeScript -> Python -> Go usage patterns and fixture scripts used in CI. -- Retry presets: packages/config/retries.ts and docs/retry-presets.md document 'low-latency', 'throughput', and 'conservative' presets used by the runtime. -- Version bumps: package.json updated to 1.8.0; docs updated. -- Tests: added unit tests for orchestrator, integration tests for streaming recovery scenarios, and CI harness changes to run cross-sdk smoke tests in the monorepo pipeline. Migration notes: existing consumer code that called internal credential helpers should switch to new orchestrator.getLeaseHint() or use the facade helper from lib/pullstream; however default behaviour preserves legacy token-provider calls so most apps will not require immediate changes. Checklist: [x] design doc linked (ENG-4321) [x] unit + integration tests [x] examples updated [x] docs + changelog entry [x] version bumps for TS/Go/Py (packaging handled in separate release PR). How tested: local unit + integration suite, nightly cross-sdk pipeline, manual verification with a sample chat app using long-running streaming sessions. CI: flakey e2e in first run (timeout in cross-language smoke) was addressed by increasing the test harness timeout and adding a small token prewarm step in the CI job. Performance: no measurable regression on small-scale benchmarks; small latency improvement observed in streaming reconnect paths due to prefetch. Rollout: merged to main and released in the next SDK patch cycle.
commits: feat(auth): compact orchestrator + prefetch hooks
feat(stream): add ergonomic pullstream facade and pub surface
chore(tests): add cross-sdk smoke harness and fixtures
docs(retry): document new retry presets and usage guidance
chore(release): bump ts sdk to 1.8.0 (placeholder changelog)
reviews: Evan Brooks (reviewer): Overall strong design. Please clarify the lifecycle guarantees for orchestrator leases (what happens on process crash).
Priya Natarajan (author reply): Added clearer doc comments and a runtime-safe best-effort lease release in orchestrator; failure does not revoke server-side leases (not in scope).
Maya Chen (reviewer): Tests look good; integration harness increase in timeout is a bit concerning. Can we gate that behind CI matrix so unit runs stay fast?
Priya Natarajan (author reply): Split pipeline so unit jobs run on default timeout and cross-sdk smoke runs on the extended matrix entry; updated ci/pipelines/cross-sdk-smoke.yml.
Diego Alvarez (reviewer): Minor API nit: pullstream.nextChunk() name feels like it leaks implementation. Suggest rename to read().
Priya Natarajan (author reply): Renamed public method to read() and left internal nextChunk for backwards compat shim; added deprecation note in README.
Evan Brooks (approval): Approving pending squash commit message cleanup and add changelog line for Py/Go bumps.
CI Bot (comment): initial pipeline failed on cross-sdk smoke (timeout); rerun requested by author.
CI Bot (comment): rerun successful; all checks green.

source.title: SDK: add experimentStickyKey option + expose assigned variant on responses
source.author: Dylan Brooks
source.description: Context: Route-level experiments require deterministic assignment. The platform supports a stickiness key (preferred: stable per end-user or per tenant) that the router uses when bucketing a request into a variant. This PR adds an optional experimentStickyKey field to the TS SDK request options and includes small helpers to read the assigned {experiment_id, variant_id} from response headers so customers can log/debug assignments. Summary of changes: 1) New request option: experimentStickyKey?: string on chat/completions/embeddings/rerank request option types. When present, the SDK sends header X-Redwood-Experiment-Sticky-Key (exact casing handled by fetch). 2) New response metadata: the SDK now captures experiment assignment headers (x-redwood-experiment-id, x-redwood-experiment-variant, x-redwood-experiment-config-version) and exposes them via response.experimental?.assignment for non-streaming calls. 3) Streaming support: for stream responses, assignment headers are available immediately from the initial response; we surface them on the returned stream handle as stream.experimental.assignment and also provide an onResponse callback hook for callers that want to log headers before consuming tokens. 4) Ergonomics: add helper parseExperimentAssignment(headers) and type ExperimentAssignment to avoid callers hand-parsing header names. 5) Docs + examples: update README snippets for route experiments usage and add a troubleshooting note (stickiness key should be high-entropy; do not default to IP; prefer customer_user_id or customer_session_id). Backwards compatibility: option is fully optional and does not change behavior when omitted. No breaking changes to existing response shapes (new fields are additive under .experimental). Testing: unit tests for header injection + parsing, and one integration-style test with mocked fetch verifying streaming handle includes assignment. Notes: header names align with platform OTel conventions and ADR-047/048; the SDK intentionally does not attempt to generate a default stickiness key to avoid low-entropy skew (see recent beta issue).
commits: feat(client): accept experimentStickyKey and send X-Redwood-Experiment-Sticky-Key header
feat(types): add ExperimentAssignment + experimental response metadata
feat(streaming): expose assignment on stream handle and add onResponse hook
test: add coverage for header parsing and request header injection
docs: update README + add route experiments snippet
changed_files: src/client/request.ts (inject experiment sticky key header; plumb onResponse)
src/types/requestOptions.ts (add experimentStickyKey to request option types)
src/streaming/stream.ts (attach experimental metadata to stream handle)
source.implementation_notes: Header contract: X-Redwood-Experiment-Sticky-Key is the only new request header added here. Response contract: SDK reads x-redwood-experiment-id, x-redwood-experiment-variant, and x-redwood-experiment-config-version. If headers are missing, assignment is undefined and the helper returns null. Streaming: because fetch exposes headers on the Response before reading the body, assignment is always available even if attribution later drops in telemetry; this is intended for client-side logging only and should not be used as a source of truth for analytics.
review_conversation: Jordan Lee: Can we confirm the stickiness header name matches what the policy engine expects? We recently standardized on X-Redwood-Experiment-Sticky-Key (not the older X-Redwood-Sticky-Key).
Dylan Brooks (author): Yep, using X-Redwood-Experiment-Sticky-Key. Added a small constant + tests to prevent regressions. Also updated README to match.
Logan Wright: Please make sure we don’t accidentally increase header cardinality in logs by default. Are we logging the stickiness key anywhere?
Dylan Brooks (author): SDK does not log headers. Also added note in docs: do not log raw stickiness keys; if needed, hash client-side.
Jada Williams: For streaming, how does a caller access the assigned variant? They won’t have the full response object.
Dylan Brooks (author): Added stream.experimental.assignment and an onResponse callback so they can log assignment before token iteration. Included a snippet in README.
Kaitlyn Nguyen: Any RBAC/security concerns with surfacing experiment_id/variant_id to customers? Should we gate it?
Dylan Brooks (author): Platform already sends these headers in beta/GA; this PR only makes them accessible. If an account isn’t enabled, headers won’t be present. No new sensitive data introduced.
Jordan Lee: Minor: parse helper should handle Header objects and plain records. Some frameworks provide a map-like interface.
Dylan Brooks (author): Updated parseExperimentAssignment to accept Headers | Record<string,string> | Array<[string,string]>. Added tests.
Jada Williams: Looks good after the streaming doc update. Approving.
Logan Wright: Approved. Thanks for keeping it additive under .experimental.
source.release_notes: TypeScript SDK: add optional experimentStickyKey request option and expose experiment assignment (experiment_id/variant_id) via response.experimental.assignment and stream.experimental.assignment for route experiments debugging.

source.title: Fix header merge for streaming in Edge runtime (preserve Authorization)
source.author: Dev Patel
source.description: Context: SUP-4884 (customer reports 403 when streaming from Next.js Edge). Root cause: in the streaming helper we constructed RequestInit twice and used object spread on headers; in Edge, Headers behaves differently and Authorization was dropped during the second merge. Changes: use a single canonical Headers instance; ensure Authorization is always set when apiKey is configured; add integration test using the edge fetch polyfill; update docs with edge runtime note. Release: v1.4.1.
commits: stream: avoid double RequestInit construction
auth: ensure Authorization header set for edge fetch
tests: edge streaming header regression test
conversation: Logan Wright: Can we reproduce without edge?
Dev Patel: Node undici path doesn't drop it; edge does due to Headers merge semantics.
Chloe Martin: Please keep workaround in ticket until release is cut.
Dev Patel: Release tag pushed.

title: Fix header merge for streaming in Edge runtime (preserve Authorization)
preview: Context: SUP-4884 (customer reports 403 when streaming from Next.js Edge). Root cause: in the streaming helper we constructed RequestInit twice and used object spread on headers; in Edge, Headers behaves differently and Authorization was dropped during the second merge. Changes: use a single canonical Headers instance; ensure Authorization is always set when apiKey is configured; add integration test using the edge fetch polyfill; update docs with edge runtime note. Release: v1.4.1.
